name: Bradshaw Mountains
short_description: BLM public land in the Bradshaw Mountains offering accessible rockhounding for quartz, agate, and chalcedony at 6,500 feet elevation with no permit required.
description:
The Bradshaw Mountains in Beaver County offer excellent public rockhounding opportunities on Bureau of Land Management land. This 6,500-foot elevation site sits in Utah's high desert transition zone where moderate slopes and scattered vegetation make collecting relatively accessible year-round for patient rockhounders.

The geology here reflects classic Basin and Range province formation. Miocene-age volcanic and hydrothermal activity deposited silica-rich fluids through fractures and faults, creating abundant quartz veins, agate nodules, and chalcedony deposits in the surrounding rock. Erosion over millions of years has exposed these minerals on hillsides and wash bottoms, making surface collecting productive without extensive digging.

Expect to find small to medium quartz crystals, translucent to milky specimens, and occasional banded agate pieces. Chalcedony appears as rounded nodules in washes. The terrain is rocky but not treacherous - hiking boots and a basic rock hammer work well here. Unlike lower desert collecting sites, the elevation keeps summer temperatures moderate, though winters can bring snow.

This is an ideal location for intermediate rockhounders and families willing to hike moderate slopes. The lack of permit requirements and 25-pound daily collection limit make it accessible for casual collectors. Bring water, sun protection, and a pack to carry specimens.
history: The Bradshaw Mountains sit within the Basin and Range physiographic province where Miocene-age volcanic activity and associated hydrothermal systems created extensive quartz and agate deposits. The mineral-rich waters percolated through fractures in surrounding rock, forming the secondary deposits rockhounders find today. Early prospectors worked surrounding areas for base metals, though quartz and agate collecting has been a minor activity in this region for decades.
tips: Search dry washes and gully bottoms first - erosion concentrates loose specimens and makes spotting easier without digging. Use a small rock hammer to crack open chalcedony nodules found in wash gravels; many contain agate banding inside dull exteriors. Focus on hillside exposures where quartz veins cut through the parent rock, looking for glassy luster and crystal terminations. Pack specimens carefully as quartz is hard but brittle along fracture planes.
terrain_notes: Moderate slopes with sparse sagebrush and juniper scrub at 6,499-foot elevation. Rocky footing throughout with exposed outcrops and scattered loose stone. Dry washes run through the area seasonally. Expect a 0.5 to 2-mile walk from parking depending on your target area. Hiking boots with good ankle support recommended due to uneven ground and loose rock.
best_season: April - May and September - October. These shoulder seasons avoid summer heat (can exceed 85F at elevation) and winter snow. Spring offers wildflowers and reliable weather. Fall brings crisp mornings and low afternoon heat. Winter is possible but snow may block access.
written_directions: From Beaver, take Highway 20 east toward Panguitch for approximately 8 miles. Turn north on a BLM access road (signs will indicate public land) and proceed 3-5 miles on an unpaved but maintained road. Look for pullouts near washes or ridge access points. Passenger vehicles can handle this in dry conditions, but high-clearance vehicles are safer after rain. The final parking areas are small pullouts; arrive early during peak season.
road_conditions: Unpaved BLM access road, washboard surface, high-clearance recommended after wet weather. Passenger vehicles suitable in dry conditions.
parking_notes: Dispersed parking at pullouts along BLM access road. No developed lot. Space for 3-5 vehicles per pullout. Arrive early. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or power tools. Hand tools only. Remove only loose or weathered specimens - do not hammer directly into bedrock or remove large outcrops.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
No damage to bedrock outcrops
nearest_services: Beaver, Utah - 12 miles west. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging. Panguitch, 20 miles northeast, also has basic services.
accessibility_notes: Moderate slopes with uneven rocky terrain. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Sturdy hiking boots required. Terrain is navigable for fit adults and older children but not suitable for limited mobility. Some routes avoid steep grades if you scout carefully.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding in the Bradshaw Mountains near Beaver, Utah?
faq.answer: You can find quartz crystals, agate nodules, chalcedony, and jasper. Quartz is the most abundant - expect milky and translucent specimens ranging from small crystals to fist-sized pieces. Chalcedony appears as rounded nodules in washes, often with agate banding visible when cracked open. Surface collecting without digging is productive here.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound in the Bradshaw Mountains, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required. This is Bureau of Land Management (BLM) public land where recreational collecting is allowed under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act. You only need to follow the 25-pound-per-day limit and collect by hand tools only - no power equipment.

faq.question: How far is the Bradshaw Mountains rockhounding site from Beaver, Utah?
faq.answer: The Bradshaw Mountains collecting areas are approximately 12 miles east of Beaver. Drive east on Highway 20 toward Panguitch for about 8 miles, then turn north on a BLM access road for 3-5 miles to reach parking pullouts. The drive takes roughly 30 minutes in good conditions.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound the Bradshaw Mountains in Utah?
faq.answer: April through May and September through October are ideal. These shoulder seasons avoid summer heat (which can exceed 85F even at 6,500-foot elevation) and winter snow that may block access. Spring offers wildflowers and stable weather; fall brings crisp mornings and cool afternoons - perfect for hiking and collecting.

faq.question: Is the Bradshaw Mountains rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, this is a beginner-friendly site. The terrain is moderate with accessible slopes, no permit required, and quartz and agate specimens are common finds for patient collectors. Kids aged 8 and up can participate. The 25-pound daily limit means you don't need extensive digging experience - surface collecting in washes and around outcrops is productive.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding in the Bradshaw Mountains?
faq.answer: Bring a small rock hammer, chisel, and hand tools - motorized equipment is not allowed on BLM land. Wear sturdy hiking boots for rocky, uneven terrain. Pack plenty of water (at least 2 liters), sunscreen, and a hat. Bring a backpack or collection bag for specimens. A headlamp is useful if you stay into dusk. Cell service is spotty, so plan accordingly.

meta_description: Find quartz, agate, and chalcedony at Bradshaw Mountains BLM land near Beaver, Utah. No permit required. 25 lbs/day limit. Beginner-friendly.
